title: Rude boy

infobox.value: * *[Blow-Up](/wiki/Blow-Up_(film) "Blow-Up (film)")* * [Casuals](/wiki/Casual_(subculture) "Casual (subculture)") * [Crew cut](/wiki/Crew_cut "Crew cut") * [Lambretta](/wiki/Lambretta "Lambretta") * [Pete Meaden](/wiki/Peter_Meaden "Peter Meaden") * [Mods and rockers](/wiki/Mods_and_rockers "Mods and rockers") * [Mod haircut](/wiki/Mod_haircut "Mod haircut") * [Pop art](/wiki/Pop_art "Pop art") * *[Quadrophenia (film)](/wiki/Quadrophenia_(film) "Quadrophenia (film)")* * Rude boy * [Scooter](/wiki/Scooter_(motorcycle) "Scooter (motorcycle)") * [Scooterboy](/wiki/Scooterboy "Scooterboy") * [Sharpies](/wiki/Sharpies_(Australian_subculture) "Sharpies (Australian subculture)") * [Skinhead](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead") * [Soulboy](/wiki/Soulboy "Soulboy") * [Suedehead](/wiki/Suedehead_(subculture) "Suedehead (subculture)") * [Swinging London](/wiki/Swinging_London "Swinging London") * [Twisted Wheel Club](/wiki/Twisted_Wheel_Club "Twisted Wheel Club") * [Youth subculture](/wiki/Youth_subculture "Youth subculture") * [Vespa](/wiki/Vespa "Vespa")

* [Ben Sherman](/wiki/Ben_Sherman "Ben Sherman") * [Beatle boots](/wiki/Beatle_boot "Beatle boot") * [Carnaby Street](/wiki/Carnaby_Street "Carnaby Street") * [Chelsea boot](/wiki/Chelsea_boot "Chelsea boot") * [Crombie coat](/wiki/Crombie_(clothing) "Crombie (clothing)") * [Fishtail parka](/wiki/Fishtail_parka "Fishtail parka") * [Fred Perry](/wiki/Fred_Perry "Fred Perry") * [Harrington](/wiki/Harrington_jacket "Harrington jacket") * [Loafers](/wiki/Loafers "Loafers") * [Miniskirt](/wiki/Miniskirt "Miniskirt") * [Nehru jacket](/wiki/Nehru_jacket "Nehru jacket") * [Mohair](/wiki/Mohair "Mohair") * [Winklepickers](/wiki/Winklepicker "Winklepicker")

is a [subculture](/wiki/Subculture "Subculture") that originated from 1960s Jamaican street culture.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In the late 1970s, there was a revival in England of the terms *rude boy* and *rude girl*, among other variations like *rudeboy* and *rudebwoy*, being used to describe fans of [two-tone](/wiki/Two-tone_(music_genre) "Two-tone (music genre)") and [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"). This revival of the subculture and term was partially the result of [Jamaican immigration to the UK](/wiki/British_Jamaican "British Jamaican") and the [so-called "Windrush" generation](/wiki/Windrush_generation "Windrush generation"). The use of these terms moved into the more contemporary [ska punk](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk") movement as well. In the UK and especially Jamaica, the terms *rude boy* and *rude girl* are used in a way similar to [gangsta](/wiki/Gangsta_rap "Gangsta rap"), [yardie](/wiki/Yardie "Yardie"), or badman.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Jamaican street subculture since 1960s

For other uses, see [Rude boy (disambiguation)](/wiki/Rude_boy_(disambiguation) "Rude boy (disambiguation)").

"Rudebwoy" redirects here. For the song by Kardinal Offishall, see [Everyday (Rudebwoy)](/wiki/Everyday_(Rudebwoy) "Everyday (Rudebwoy)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Prince_Buster.jpg/250px-Prince_Buster.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prince_Buster.jpg)

[Prince Buster](/wiki/Prince_Buster "Prince Buster") performing at the Cardiff Festival, Cardiff, UK

**Rude boy** is a [subculture](/wiki/Subculture "Subculture") that originated from 1960s Jamaican street culture.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In the late 1970s, there was a revival in England of the terms *rude boy* and *rude girl*, among other variations like *rudeboy* and *rudebwoy*, being used to describe fans of [two-tone](/wiki/Two-tone_(music_genre) "Two-tone (music genre)") and [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"). This revival of the subculture and term was partially the result of [Jamaican immigration to the UK](/wiki/British_Jamaican "British Jamaican") and the [so-called "Windrush" generation](/wiki/Windrush_generation "Windrush generation"). The use of these terms moved into the more contemporary [ska punk](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk") movement as well. In the UK and especially Jamaica, the terms *rude boy* and *rude girl* are used in a way similar to [gangsta](/wiki/Gangsta_rap "Gangsta rap"), [yardie](/wiki/Yardie "Yardie"), or badman.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Jamaica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rude_boy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Jamaica")]

The term *rude boy*, and the rude boy subculture, arose from the poorer sections of [Kingston, Jamaica](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"), and was associated with violent discontented youths.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Along with [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady") music, many rude boys favored sharp suits, thin ties, and [pork pie](/wiki/Pork_pie_hat "Pork pie hat") or [Trilby](/wiki/Trilby "Trilby") hats, showing an influence of the fashions of US [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") musicians and [soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") artists. It is also believed that the rude boy style was influenced by Sean Connery's sharp, tight suits worn when he portrayed James Bond, testified to by several ska songs that made reference to James Bond. Well-known cowboy and gangster/outlaw films from that period were also influential factors in shaping the rude boy image, as scholars like Rob Wilson, Christopher Leigh Connory, and [Deborah A. Thomas](/wiki/Deborah_A._Thomas "Deborah A. Thomas") have shown.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) In that time period, unemployed Jamaican youths sometimes found temporary employment from [sound system](/wiki/Sound_system_(Jamaican) "Sound system (Jamaican)") operators to disrupt competitors' dances (leading to the term *dancehall crasher*).[[6]](#cite_note-6) The violence that sometimes occurred at dances and its association with the rude boy lifestyle gave rise to a slew of releases by artists who addressed the rude boys directly with lyrics that either promoted or rejected rude boy violence, for example the 1967 song "[Rudy a Message to You](/wiki/A_Message_to_You_Rudy "A Message to You Rudy")" by [Dandy Livingstone](/wiki/Dandy_Livingstone "Dandy Livingstone").

Starting in the 1970s, Jamaican [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") music replaced the ska and rocksteady music associated with the rude boys. In the 1980s, [dancehall](/wiki/Dancehall "Dancehall") became the main Jamaican popular music genre, drawing some parallels with the earlier rude boys in its culture and lyrical content.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rude_boy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Rude_boys%2C_Notting_Hill_1974_%281922313268%29.jpg/250px-Rude_boys%2C_Notting_Hill_1974_%281922313268%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rude_boys,_Notting_Hill_1974_(1922313268).jpg)

Rude Boys in [Notting Hill](/wiki/Notting_Hill "Notting Hill"), London (1974)

In the 1960s, the [Jamaican diaspora](/wiki/Jamaican_diaspora "Jamaican diaspora") introduced rude boy music and fashion to the United Kingdom, which influenced the [mod](/wiki/Mod_(subculture) "Mod (subculture)") and [skinhead](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead") subcultures.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) In the late 1970s, the term *rude boy* and rude boy fashions came back into use after the [2 tone](/wiki/2_Tone_(music_genre) "2 Tone (music genre)") band [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials") (notably with a cover of "[A Message to You Rudy](/wiki/A_Message_to_You_Rudy "A Message to You Rudy")") and their record label [2 Tone Records](/wiki/2_Tone_Records "2 Tone Records") instigated a brief but influential ska revival.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In this same spirit, [the Clash](/wiki/The_Clash "The Clash") contributed "[Rudie Can't Fail](/wiki/Rudie_Can%27t_Fail "Rudie Can't Fail")" on their 1979 album *[London Calling](/wiki/London_Calling "London Calling")*, and [The Ruts](/wiki/The_Ruts "The Ruts") their 1980 single "Staring at the Rude Boys". In more recent times in multicultural Britain, the term *rudeboy* has become associated with street or urban culture, and is a common greeting.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The term *rudeboy* has become associated with music genres such as [ragga](/wiki/Ragga "Ragga"), [jungle](/wiki/Oldschool_jungle "Oldschool jungle"), [drum and bass](/wiki/Drum_and_bass "Drum and bass"), [UK garage](/wiki/UK_garage "UK garage"), and [grime](/wiki/Grime_music "Grime music") – although is still used by many [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and [ska punk](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk") bands, old and new – predominantly in the UK and USA.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rude_boy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: See also")]
